coredata.dc:title: Co-sensitization of TiO2 electrode with Eosin Y dye and carbon dots for photoelectrochemical water splitting: The enhanced dye adsorption and the charge transfer route
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2017 Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Sang, Lixia
Lei, Lei
Lin, Jia
Ge, Hao
coredata.dc:description: According to a stepwise adsorption approach, TiO2 NT photoanode was co-sensitized with Eosin Y and carbon dots for photoelectrochemical water splitting. The adsorption amount of Eosin Y in co-sensitized samples increases due to the presence of carbon dots regardless of co-sensitization sequence. The absorbance of Eosin Y/carbon dots/TiO2 NT in visible light region is stronger than that of carbon dots/Eosin Y/TiO2 NT, while carbon dots/Eosin Y/TiO2 NT exhibits higher photocurrent density and hydrogen production rate. Based on the shortest electron transport time, the electron diffusion coefficient in carbon dots/Eosin Y/TiO2 NT electrode is up to 14.94 × 10−19cm2s−1. A favorable energy levels alignment dominates in carbon dots/Eosin Y/TiO2 NT, which can further account for enhancement in charge transfer.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Eosin Y
TiO2
Co-sensitization
Photoelectrochemical water splitting

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of carbon dots sensitized TiO2 NT electrodes
Preparation of Eosin Y sensitized TiO2 NT electrodes
Preparation of Co-sensitized TiO2 NT electrode
Photoelectrochemical activity and hydrogen evolution measurements

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2017 Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of (a,b) TiO2 NT and sensitized photoanodes ((c) carbon dots/TiO2 NT, (d) Eosin Y/TiO2 NT, (e) EosinY/carbon dots/TiO2 NT, (f) carbon dots/EosinY/TiO2 NT). The insets of (e) and (f) show the cross-sectional morphologies of the corresponding samples.
UV-Vis DRS spectra of TiO2 NT (a), carbon dots/TiO2 NT(b), Eosin Y/TiO2 NT(c), Eosin Y/carbon dots/TiO2 NT(d), and carbon dots/Eosin Y/TiO2 NT(e).

I-t plots of TiO2 NT electrodes (a), carbon dots/TiO2 NT (b), Eosin Y/TiO2 NT(c), Eosin Y/carbon dots/TiO2 NT (d), and carbon dots/EosinY/TiO2 NT (e) at 0 V vs Ag/AgCl. Electrolyte: 0.5M Na2SO4 and 15 vol% TEOA aqueous solution. Irradiation: 100 mW cm−2, AM 1.5G.

Typical IMPS response of TiO2 NT electrode and the sensitized TiO2 NT electrodes with the same light intensity (I o = 90 mW cm−2, λ = 365 nm). 0.5M Na2SO4 and 15 vol% TEOA aqueous solution was served as electrolyte.

Electron transfer in model I (a) and model II (b) based on energy levels of TiO2, Eosin Y, and carbon dots. TiO2 has the conduction band (CB) edge and valence band (VB) edge potential of -4.0 and -7.2 eV vs Vacuum, respectively [10]. The LUMO and HOMO of Eosin Y dye molecule is positioned at -3.65 and -5.85 eV vs Vacuum, respectively [36]. The LUMO and HOMO levels of carbon dots lie at -3.2 and -5.9 eV vs Vacuum, respectively [33].
The adsorption amount of Eosin Y on the sensitized TiO2 photoanodes.
Photocurrent densities and hydrogen production rates of the TiO2 NT and sensitized TiO2 NT electrodes in PEC under one sun irradiation with a solar simulator. a

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Applied bias of 0 V; geometrical area of photoelectrode is 1 cm2.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: c
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Photocurrent density is obtained by I-t plots.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Electron transport time (τ d) and electron diffusion coefficient (D n) of TiO2 NT and sensitized TiO2 NT electrodes in PEC (I o = 90 mW cm−2, λ = 365 nm). a

head.ce:title: Co-sensitization of TiO2 electrode with Eosin Y dye and carbon dots for photoelectrochemical water splitting: The enhanced dye adsorption and the charge transfer route

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Enhanced Heat Transfer and Energy Conservation of Ministry of Education, Key Laboratory of Heat Transfer and Energy Conservation Beijing Municipality, Department of Energy Science and Engineering, College of Environmental and Energy Engineering, Beijing University of Technology, Beijing 100124, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Enhanced Heat Transfer and Energy Conservation of Ministry of Education
Key Laboratory of Heat Transfer and Energy Conservation Beijing Municipality
Department of Energy Science and Engineering
College of Environmental and Energy Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: According to a stepwise adsorption approach, TiO2 NT photoanode was co-sensitized with Eosin Y and carbon dots for photoelectrochemical water splitting. The adsorption amount of Eosin Y in co-sensitized samples increases due to the presence of carbon dots regardless of co-sensitization sequence. The absorbance of Eosin Y/carbon dots/TiO2 NT in visible light region is stronger than that of carbon dots/Eosin Y/TiO2 NT, while carbon dots/Eosin Y/TiO2 NT exhibits higher photocurrent density and hydrogen production rate. Based on the shortest electron transport time, the electron diffusion coefficient in carbon dots/Eosin Y/TiO2 NT electrode is up to 14.94 × 10−19cm2s−1. A favorable energy levels alignment dominates in carbon dots/Eosin Y/TiO2 NT, which can further account for enhancement in charge transfer.

ce:section.ce:para: Photoelectrochemical water splitting is a promising route for solar energy storage and hydrogen production. TiO2 has been widely studied as photoanode because of its photochemical stability, nontoxicity and low cost [1–3]. However, the energy conversion efficiency of TiO2 is low due to the large band gap (3.2 eV) and the rapid electron-hole recombination. To extend the optical absorption to visible-light region and promote the separation of the photogenerated electron-hole pairs, the sensitizers such as the organic dyes [4–7] or the narrow band gap semiconductors [8,9] are often used. Recently, co-sensitization is a new approach to expand largely the optical absorption range through the combination of two or more sensitizers. Ruthenium-based dye C106 and organic D131 sensitizers with the complementary absorption properties and different molecular sizes have been judicially chosen for co-sensitization, which yields a higher light-harvesting efficiency as well as better dye coverage to passivate the surface of TiO2 [10]. Eosin Y (EY) belongs to the xanthene dye, which has been used as dye sensitizer of TiO2 for the photocatalytic hydrogen evolution [11–13]. To enhance the light harvesting capacity of dye-sensitized TiO2, the loading amount of dye is needed to be increased. Another issue is the stability of dye in applications of sensitization system. Li et al. [14] found that an average apparent quantum efficiency over EY sensitized CuO/TiO2 can be up to 5.1% in a water splitting system of diethylamine (DEA) solution under visible light irradiation (λ > 420 nm). The addition of CuO can strongly improve the adsorption capability of TiO2 toward EY dye by the formation of multidentate complexation. Liu et al. [15] reported that EY sensitized SiW11/TiO2 exhibits an average quantum efficiency of 11.4% during irradiation (λ > 420 nm) of 20 h. SiW11 as an excellent electron relay greatly facilitates the electron transfer from the reduced dye species EY.- to conduction band (CB) of TiO2 and suppresses the decomposition of EY.-.

Notably, carbon dots can increase the light absorbance and improve the solar-hydrogen conversion efficiency as a novel environmentally friendly sensitizer [16,17]. Many research groups have widely focused on designing some new nanocomposites such as carbon dots/ZnO [18], carbon dots/Fe2O3 [19], carbon dots/Ag3PO4 [20], carbon dots/TiO2 [21] to enhance the photocatalytic activity. As for carbon dots/Ag3PO4 [20], carbon dots can not only protect Ag3PO4 from dissolution in aqueous solution but also act as both electron acceptors and donors to improve the charge transfer properties. Inspired by light absorption and resonance energy transfer processes of chlorophyll, Ma et al. [22] introduced carbon dots into the conventional dye-sensitized semiconductor systems, which show significantly higher photoelectric conversion efficiency, as much as 7 times that without carbon dots. Carbon dots could act as a one-way electron transfer intermediary for effective bridging of Rh B molecules and TiO2 substrate, thereby developing highly efficient photoelectric devices. The findings also give us the idea of combining carbon dots with dye to construct electrode for solar-hydrogen conversion.

As stated above, both carbon dots and Eosin Y can improve the performance of TiO2 electrode for water splitting. However, to the best of our knowledge, there is little research on co-sensitization of TiO2 electrode with carbon dots and Eosin Y dye. During co-sensitization process, first and foremost, it needs the matched energy levels of the sensitizers. In Souza's group work [23], when N719 dye is anchored between TiO2 and CdSe quantum dots, CdSe/N719/TiO2 electrode exhibits the cell efficiency of 2.64%. While CdSe quantum dots are sandwiched between TiO2 and N719 dye, the cell efficiency of N719/CdSe/TiO2 electrode is only 1.16%. It is attributed to charge transfer effectively in a stepwise structure formed by the re-organization of energy levels. Therefore, when choosing carbon dots and Eosin Y as the co-sensitizers, it should clarify the effects of the charge transfer route in co-sensitization systems on the photoelectrochemical water splitting performances.

In our previous work [24], we have obtained the better preparation condition of carbon dots via electrochemical ablation of graphite rods and investigated the photoelectrochemical hydrogen activities of the carbon dots/TiO2 nanotube arrays (TiO2 NT) electrodes. Herein, we focus on fabricating the TiO2 NT electrodes co-sensitized with carbon dots and Eosin Y to further improve the photoelectrochemical performances. More efforts are to analyze the interfacial properties and the electron transport kinetic parameters of the co-sensitized TiO2 NT electrode. During the preparation, the sensitization sequence of carbon dots and Eosin Y is investigated. Accordingly, the possible charge transfer routes in as-prepared samples are discussed.

Titanium foils (0.25 mm thickness, 99.5% purity) were supplied by Alfa-Aesar Chemical Technology Co. Ltd. Phosphate acid (H3PO4, ≥85% in water), triethanolamine(TEOA, ≥99% in water), sodium fluoride (NaF, ≥98%) and sodium sulfate (Na2SO4, ≥98%), were purchased from Tianjin Chemical Reagent Company. Ethanol (AR) and acetone (AR) were purchased from Beijing Chemical Corporation. All solutions were prepared using high-purity deionized water. Alcohol-soluble Eosin Y (C20H8Br4O5) was acquired from Sigma Aldrich Co. Ltd.

Two graphite rods (99.99%, Tianjin Ai Da technology Co. Ltd) were perpendicularly inserted in a 100 ml beaker containing a mixture of deionized water and ethanol with 0.4 g NaOH [25,26], and they were used as anode and cathode with a separation of 3.5 cm, respectively. A direct current (DC) power supply was applied to provide the potential of 40 V between the two electrodes for 5 h. During the process, the solution quickly changes from colorless to yellow and finally to dark brown. Afterwards, the solution was filtrated to remove impurities and large particles, resulting in carbon dots solution.

TiO2 nanotube array (TiO2 NT) electrode was prepared by anodic oxidation method according to our previous report [27]. The anodization of Ti foil was carried out in an aqueous solution of 0.5M H3PO4 and 0.14 M NaF for in ultrasonication irradiation (Kunshan Ultrasonic Instrument Co. Ltd., KQ-100DE). The platinum gauze (20 mm × 30 mm) was counter electrode and 20 V DC voltage was supplied by DC power (Maynuo M8812, 0–2 A, 0–75 V) at room temperature for 1 h. After anodization, samples were properly washed with deionized water immediately to remove absorbed ions, and annealed at 450 °C for 4 h in air.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of carbon dots sensitized TiO2 NT electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: The carbon dots were directly grown on the TiO2 NT electrodes surfaces by impregnation method [24]. The experimental process is as follows: the prepared TiO2 NT electrodes were immersed into carbon dots solution for 18 h, followed by rinsing with deionized water to remove the remaining carbon dots solution from the electrodes surface, and finally dried in air at room temperature. The resulting sample was designated as carbon dots/TiO2 NT electrode.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of Eosin Y sensitized TiO2 NT electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: Eosin Y sensitization [12] was performed by soaking the dried TiO2 NT samples in 3 mM Eosin Y dye ethanol solution at 40 °C for 12 h. The electrodes were drained from the reaction solution and washed with ethanol. The resulting sample was designated as Eosin Y/TiO2 NT electrode.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of Co-sensitized TiO2 NT electrode
ce:section.ce:para: The resulting carbon dots/TiO2 NT electrode was dipped in 3 mM Eosin Y dye ethanol solution at 40 °C for 12 h. The co-sensitized electrode was washed with ethanol. The resulting sample was designated as Eosin Y/carbon dots/TiO2 NT electrode. For comparison, TiO2 NT electrode was firstly soaked in 3 mM Eosin Y dye ethanol solution at 40 °C for 12 h, and then dipped in carbon dots solution for 18 h. After washed with ethanol, the obtained sample was designated as carbon dots/Eosin Y/TiO2 NT electrode.

The surface morphologies of as-prepared samples were characterized with a field-emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM; Hitachi, Japan, S4300 or S4800; JEOL JSM 6500 F) at 10–15 kV accelerating voltage. The ultraviolet-visible diffuse reflectance spectra of the samples were measured by an ultraviolet-visible spectrometer (UV-Vis DRS; Avantes, Netherlands, Avaspec-2048L) and compressed BaSO4 powder as standard. To quantify the dye loading on the TiO2 NT electrode surface, we immersed the sensitized electrodes into a 0.1M NaOH solution in a mixed solvent (water: ethanol = 1:1, in volume fraction). The amounts of Eosin Y desorbed were calculated by measuring the absorption spectra of NaOH solution and monitoring the peak at 515 nm [24].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photoelectrochemical activity and hydrogen evolution measurements
ce:section.ce:para: The photoelectrochemical (PEC) experiments were carried out in a three-electrode system with a platinum gauze (20 mm × 30 mm) counter electrode, a saturated Ag/AgCl reference electrode and the prepared sample working electrode in 0.5M Na2SO4 and 15 vol% TEOA solution. A solar simulator (91160, Newport, USA) with an AM 1.5 filter was used as light source, adjusting the light intensity to 100 mW cm−2. The intensity modulated photocurrent spectrum (IMPS) measurements were performed under short-circuit conditions [28,29], while the cell was under background illumination was from a modulated light of 90 mW cm−2 at 365 nm. All the photoelectrochemical properties data were scanned and recorded three times to guarantee the reliability on the electrochemical analyzer (Autolab 302N, Switzerland). The production of hydrogen was online detected by a gas-chromatograph (Agilent 7890B, 5 A molecular sieve, N2 carrier gas).

The SEM images of TiO2 NT, carbon dots sensitized TiO2 NT, Eosin Y sensitized TiO2 NT and carbon dots/Eosin Y co-sensitized TiO2 NTs are shown in Fig. 1 . From Fig. 1a and b, it can be found that the as-prepared TiO2 NT has an outer average diameter of 100 nm and a tube length of 550 nm. The one-dimensional structure TiO2 NT allows photo-generated electrons to move vertically along nanotubes, providing directly and faster electrons to the titanium substrate. When TiO2 NT is sensitized by Eosin Y and carbon dots (Fig. 1c–f), the one-dimensional tubular structure of TiO2 NT is unchanged. In order to obtain the chemisorbed Eosin Y on TiO2 NT, the sensitized samples were rinsed to remove the physical absorption of Eosin Y during the preparation. Table 1 shows the amount of the chemisorbed Eosin Y on the TiO2 NT surface. Obviously, the adsorption amount of dye on co-sensitized TiO2 NT electrodes is higher than that of Eosin Y/TiO2 electrode, which is largely attributed to the presence of carbon dots. There are some hydroxyls on the surface of carbon dots. The hydroxyl groups may play a significant role in adsorbing Eosin Y dye molecules [30]. The hydroxyl group and the carboxy group of dye can be bonded by dehydration reaction, resulting in the more chemisorbed dye. It is well known that the dye concentration on TiO2 can proportionately enhance the performance of the TiO2 film based device. It is reported that more dye adsorption in TiO2/N719 dye-CdSe is the reason for more photocurrent in the cell [23]. In addition, the amounts of dye adsorbed on carbon dots/Eosin Y/TiO2 NT is up to the highest value of 0.122 mmol cm−2. And the difference in the adsorption amount between carbon dots/Eosin Y/TiO2 NT and Eosin Y/carbon dots/TiO2 NT is only 0.003 mmol cm−2. It means that the sensitization sequence of carbon dots and Eosin Y has a little influence on adsorption of Eosin Y on TiO2 NT.

Fig. 2 depicts the UV-Vis adsorption spectra of the TiO2 NT with and without different sensitizers. As shown in Fig. 2(a), the pure TiO2 NT has great ability to respond to the light less than 400 nm. For carbon dots/TiO2 NT of Fig. 2(b), it shows a combined absorption band of carbon dots and pure TiO2 NT, and the absorption band in visible light region is originated from carbon dots. Besides the band edge of TiO2 NT, the absorption peak of Eosin Y/TiO2 NT at about 515 nm belongs to Eosin Y (Fig. 2(c)) [31]. The absorption of Eosin Y/carbon dots/TiO2 NT is similar to that of Eosin Y/TiO2 NT, but the absorption peak at about 515 nm become stronger due to more Eosin Y molecules attached to the TiO2 surface (Fig. 2 (d)). In the case of carbon dots/Eosin Y/TiO2 NT (Fig. 2(e)), it is a little difficult to find the band edge of TiO2 NT and the characteristic absorption peak of Eosin Y. The enhanced adsorption, especially in the wavelength range of 400–650 nm, is more related to carbon dots, indicating an effective interaction between carbon dots, Eosin Y and TiO2 NT by functional groups. Obviously, carbon dots and Eosin Y can extend the light response of the TiO2 NT into the visible region, and the co-sensitized samples exhibit the enhanced light absorption as compared with the individual sensitizer loaded TiO2 NT. Therefore, it is expected that the co-sensitized TiO2 NT electrodes will present better photoelectrochemical performances due to their improved light-harvesting efficiency.

Fig. 3 shows the photocurrent-time curves for TiO2 NT and TiO2 NT electrodes with different sensitizer in 0.5M Na2SO4 and 15 vol% TEOA aqueous solution. In the process of photoelectrochemical reaction, TEOA is used as an electron donor [32], which can not only regenerate the oxidized dye but also recombine with the photo-generated holes to improve the collection efficiency of photo-generated electrons. Under the interrupted illumination, the chopped photocurrent response can reflect the ability to generate electrons of photoanode. As can be seen from Fig. 3, the photocurrent density of TiO2 NT, carbon dots/TiO2 NT, Eosin Y/TiO2 NT, Eosin Y/carbon dots/TiO2 NT, and carbon dots/Eosin Y/TiO2 NT electrodes is rapidly approaching to 0.33, 0.39, 0.42, 0.44, and 0.49 mA/cm2, respectively. The photocurrent density of Eosin Y/TiO2 NT electrode is slightly higher than that of carbon dots/TiO2 NT electrode. At this point, Eosin Y may be the primary sensitizer in co-sensitization system. So, we analyze the adsorption amount of Eosin Y on the co-sensitized samples in Section Surface morphology and optical properties. In addition, the photocurrent density of co-sensitized TiO2 NT electrodes is higher than that of Eosin Y/TiO2 NT or carbon dots/TiO2 NT electrode, indicating the remarkable co-sensitization effect.

In the three-electrode system for water splitting, the hydrogen is evolved from Pt cathode. The hydrogen production data were recorded by using a gas chromatograph, which are given in Table 2 . After 4 h of the simulated sunlight irradiation, the mean hydrogen production rates of pure TiO2 NT, carbon dots/TiO2 NT, Eosin Y/TiO2 NT, Eosin Y/carbon dots/TiO2 NT, and carbon dots/Eosin Y/TiO2 NT electrodes are ∼108, 127, 137, 144, 160 μL/h·cm−2, respectively. After the reaction, the SEM images of photoanodes show that their morphologies remain the one-dimensional tubular structure. Moreover, in the process of the hydrogen evolution measurements, the photocurrent was recorded at the same time by the electrochemical analyzer. The obtained photocurrent densities are steady within the testing time. It implies that the resulting photoanodes remain unchanged during the photoelectrochemical water splitting reaction. Shown in Table 2, the trend of hydrogen production rate is carbon dots/Eosin Y/TiO2 NT > Eosin Y/carbon dots/TiO2 NT > Eosin Y/TiO2 NT > carbon dots/TiO2 NT > TiO2 NT, which is consistent with that of the photocurrent density at 0 V vs Ag/AgCl. It also can be found that the hydrogen generation rate of co-sensitized electrodes is much higher than that of individual sensitized electrode. Furthermore, the carbon dots/Eosin Y/TiO2 NT electrode had a much higher evolution rate which depends on the improved light-harvesting ability as well as its interfacial charge transfer property.

To study electron transfer kinetics in our system, we performed the intensity modulated photocurrent spectrum (IMPS) experiments. Fig. 4 shows complex plane plots of IMPS for the TiO2 NT electrodes before and after sensitization.

As shown in Fig. 4, the IMPS plots display the semicircles in complex plane. The electron transport time (τ d) can be estimated from the bottom of the semicircle using the eqn. (1) [33]:
(1)
τ d
=
(
2 π
f
min
)
− 1
where f min is the corresponding frequency at the bottom of the IMPS semicircle. The electron diffusion coefficient (D n) also can be estimated as shown in eqn. (2) [34]:
(2)
D n
= α ·
d 2
/
τ d
where α = 0.393 [34] is the proportionality coefficient, d is the TiO2 nanotube length. The electron transport time (τ d) and electron diffusion coefficient (D n) recorded for TiO2 NT, carbon dots/TiO2 NT, Eosin Y/TiO2 NT, Eosin Y/carbon dots/TiO2 NT, and carbon dots/Eosin Y/TiO2 NT electrodes in PEC are given in Table 3 .

As shown in Table 3, compared with pure TiO2 NT electrode, the electron transport time (τ d) for the sensitized TiO2 NT electrode is decreased. The electron transport time decreases in the order of TiO2 NT > carbon dots/TiO2 NT > Eosin Y/TiO2 NT > Eosin Y/carbon dots/TiO2 NT > carbon dots/Eosin Y/TiO2 NT, which is consistent with the trend of variation in photocurrent density. Obviously, the co-sensitized samples exhibit much shorter electron transport time than carbon dots/TiO2 NT or Eosin Y/TiO2 NT electrodes. The shorter electron transport time indicates the higher electron transport rate. Moreover, the carbon dots/Eosin Y/TiO2 NT electrode gives the shortest electron transport time of 0.16 ms, which is a half of that of carbon dots/Eosin Y/TiO2 NT electrode (0.32 ms). It suggests that carbon dots/Eosin Y/TiO2 NT electrode has the better electron transport properties.

Based on electron transport time, the electron diffusion coefficient (D n) can be obtained as shown in Table 3. It is known that the electron diffusion coefficient (D n) depends on the concentration gradient of photogenerated electrons and the probability of electrons trapped by defects [35]. In other words, the value of D n reflects how hard the electron transports in the concentration gradient. From Table 3, the sensitized samples have the relative great electron diffusion coefficient than the sample without sensitization. Owing to the shortest electron transport time, the carbon dots/Eosin Y/TiO2 NT electrode exhibits the highest electron diffusion coefficient of 14.94 × 10−19cm2s−1. On one hand, as analyzed in Section Surface morphology and optical properties, the better surface coverage of sensitizer will lead to the enhanced light absorption and more photogenerated electrons. On the other hand, the favorable energy levels in carbon dots/Eosin Y/TiO2 NT electrode may further account for enhancement in charge transfer.

The objective of co-sensitization is to further enhance device performance by having complementary absorption spectra and more carrier generation. In the present work, both co-sensitized TiO2 NT electrodes exhibit the improved photon absorption in visible light region. Under irradiation, more electrons can be excited from Eosin Y dye and carbon dots. Although the absorbance of Eosin Y/carbon dots/TiO2 NT in visible light portion is stronger than that of carbon dots/Eosin Y/TiO2 NT with similar absorption amounts of Eosin Y, the photocurrent density and the hydrogen production rate of the later are higher than those of the former. To understand the difference in photoelectrochemical performances of both co-sensitized TiO2 NT electrodes with different sensitization sequence, the energy diagram of TiO2 and sensitizers has been drawn to explain the charge transfer mechanism as shown in Fig. 5 .

Since the LUMO (lowest unoccupied molecular orbital) levels of both Eosin Y dye molecule (-3.65 eV vs Vacuum) [36] and carbon dots (-3.2 eV vs Vacuum) [37] lie above the conduction band edge of TiO2 (-4.0 eV vs Vacuum) [10], the electron injection from the photo-excited dye and carbon dots to TiO2 is thermodynamically favorable. According to the stepwise co-sensitization approach, there are two models for charge transfer in both co-sensitized samples. The charge transfer in both models can effectively facilitate charge separation. Consequently, the co-sensitized samples have the better performances than its individual constituent. Here, we pay more attention to electron transfer in interface region. For Model I as shown in Fig. 5a, the alignment of energy levels can provide the driving force for a better electron mobility and charge separation. More photogenerated electrons descend form carbon dots via Eosin Y dye to TiO2, contributing to an overall enhanced photocurrent. In the case of Model II, carbon dots are sandwiched between Eosin Y dye and TiO2 (Fig. 5b), the directional charge transfer will be weakened because the LUMO of Eosin Y dye is below the LUMO of carbon dots. The electrons will have choice to move to TiO2 or to Eosin Y dye from carbon dots, at the same time, the electrons from Eosin Y dye to TiO2 may be blocked. Based on the sequential co-sensitization method, a preferred charge transfer in terms of Model I dominates in carbon dots/Eosin Y/TiO2 NT, while charge transfer in Eosin Y/carbon dots/TiO2 NT mainly depends on Model II. A little unfavorable electron transfer will lead to the reduction of performance. This is one of reasons for the difference in performances between carbon dots/Eosin Y/TiO2 NT and Eosin Y/carbon dots/TiO2 NT electrodes for photoelectrochemical water splitting.

The focus of our work is on co-sensitization of TiO2 NT photoanode with Eosin Y and carbon dots to maximize the light-harvesting efficiency and the hydrogen evolution rate in photoelectrochemical water splitting system. According to a stepwise co-sensitization approach, Eosin Y/carbon dots/TiO2 NT and carbon dots/Eosin Y/TiO2 NT were fabricated with inverse adsorption sequence. It is found that carbon dots can effectively enhance the adsorption of Eosin Y on the electrode surface. The adsorption amount of Eosin Y remarkably increases in co-sensitized samples, and the co-sensitization sequence has little influence on loading amount of Eosin Y. Both co-sensitized TiO2 NT electrodes exhibit the improved photon absorption in visible light region, while the absorbance of Eosin Y/carbon dots/TiO2 NT in visible light region is stronger than that of carbon dots/Eosin Y/TiO2 NT. However, carbon dots/Eosin Y/TiO2 NT shows higher photocurrent density and hydrogen production rate. From the intensity modulated photocurrent spectra (IMPS), it can be revealed that the sensitization can shorten the electron transport time and enlarge the electron diffusion coefficient. In particular, the electron diffusion coefficient in carbon dots/Eosin Y/TiO2 NT electrode is up to 14.94 × 10−19cm2s−1. There are two charge transfer models in the co-sensitized electrode interface based on energy levels of TiO2, Eosin Y, and carbon dots. More photogenerated electrons descend form carbon dots via Eosin Y dye to TiO2, contributing to an overall enhanced photocurrent. When carbon dots are sandwiched between Eosin Y dye and TiO2, the directional charge transfer will be weakened, leading to the reduction of performances. Consequently, a preferred charge transfer in terms of charge separation dominates in carbon dots/Eosin Y/TiO2 NT. The findings would be of great importance to design and develop the high-efficiency photoelectrode for solar-hydrogen conversion.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No.51376013,51776009) for their financial support.
